data.title: Axalta downgraded at UBS as Akzo deal tempers near-term upside
data.description:
UBS on Monday downgraded Axalta Coating Systems (AXTA) to Neutral from Buy, citing a more balanced risk-reward profile following the proposed acquisition by Akzo Nobel (AKZOF) (AKZOY) and limited near-term catalysts for the stock.

In a note dated Jan. 12, UBS analyst Joshua Spector lowered his price target on Axalta shares to $35 from $39, arguing that much of the takeover optimism is already reflected in the stock after a roughly 15% rally since the deal was announced.

Deal logic intact but benefits pushed out

UBS said the strategic rationale for combining Axalta (AXTA) and Akzo remains sound, particularly given the potential for cost savings that could support earnings growth for both companies. However, those synergies are unlikely to materialize for several years, with meaningful benefits not expected until closer to 2028.

While UBS estimates the combined company could eventually unlock more than $600 million in annual cost savings, the firm said little of that upside is currently being priced in by investors. The pro forma valuation implies only modest upside for Axalta shares in a deal-completion scenario, according to the report.

Stand-alone outlook remains muted

Outside the transaction, UBS sees limited organic growth prospects for Axalta in the medium term. The firm expects only low single-digit earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization growth through 2026 and does not forecast a full recovery in the company’s Refinish coatings business until the second half of that year.

UBS warned that if investors begin to view Axalta (AXTA) as a no-growth story, the stock could trade at a lower valuation multiple, potentially pressuring shares despite the company’s improving free cash flow profile

Cash builds but deployment uncertain

Axalta (AXTA) is expected to generate substantial free cash flow over the next two years, but UBS noted that capital deployment is effectively on hold while the company awaits clarity on the Akzo transaction. In a deal-break scenario, UBS said Axalta (AXTA) would have flexibility to deploy cash, but investor confidence could weaken until management outlines a longer-term strategy.

Given these factors, UBS said the stock now offers limited upside relative to downside risk, leading to the downgrade to Neutral.

data.title: Bill Pulte denies role in Fed subpoenas in a recent Bloomberg interview
data.description:
Federal Housing Finance Agency Director Bill Pulte used a tense Bloomberg interview on Monday to firmly deny any role in the Justice Department’s surprise subpoenas to the Federal Reserve, calling the investigation “outside of my purview” and directing all questions to DOJ.

The exchange came after a report that described him as a driving force behind the move against Fed Chair Jerome Powell, but Pulte declined to discuss whether he had spoken with President Trump or other senior officials about the subpoenas, refusing to reveal any private conversations from a recent Air Force One trip.

Pulte steered the conversation toward housing policy, casting himself and Trump as leading an aggressive push to lower mortgage costs and make homes more affordable for ordinary Americans.

He hailed a “historic” plan for Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac to buy around $200 billion in mortgage‑backed securities and argued that rates had already begun to fall in response.

He also painted a turnaround narrative at the two mortgage giants, saying the administration had dragged them out of a remote‑work stupor and was now using them more assertively to support homeownership.

The investigation into the Fed reportedly centers on the central bank’s renovation of its Washington headquarters and whether Powell lied to Congress about the project’s scope, according to the New York Times.

data.title: Albemarle, Lithium Argentina hit new 52-week highs as Scotiabank upgrades
data.description:
Albemarle (ALB) +4.2% and Lithium Argentina (LAR) +7.1% to new multi-year highs in Monday's trading as both lithium producers were upgraded at Scotiabank to Sector Outperform from Sector Perform with respective $200 and $7.75 price targets, joining SQM (SQM) with the same rating and saying the current lithium rally is "only the first leg, in what should be a multi-year tightening cycle."

Scotiabank's Ben Isaacson said most lithium market balance outcomes foreseen by the bank point to a supply-constrained market mid-term, and "even if electric vehicle demand growth slightly misses expectations, or even if BESS does not soar by leaps and bounds, a market inflection appears well-supported."

The analyst forecast lithium demand reaching 2.8M metric tons in 2030, with a bear-to-bull range of 2.5M-3.2M mt, which would imply a five-year compound annual growth rate of ~14% in his base case and a bear-to-bull range of 12%-17%.

Isaacson said concern over Albemarle's (ALB) balance sheet is fading, with a fourth consecutive quarter of leverage improvement; the combination of operating expense cost-cutting and capital spending cost-cutting should see the company exit 2025 with positive free cash flow of $300M-$400M; and as inflection point visibility improves, coupled with continued progress on self-help initiatives, the stock has the potential to rally meaningfully higher.

"Watch for sharp mid-term earnings revisions higher in ‘26, as data-driven confidence of a tightening lithium market accelerates," Isaacson wrote, adding that for many North American generalists, Alemarle (ALB) is "the only viable large cap name they can use to express lithium views."

Regarding Lithium Argentina (LAR), Isaacson noted Cauchari produced 34,100 metric tons of lithium carbonate, in line with guidance and reflecting an 85% operating rate for the year; with cash operating costs now below $6K/mt, the operation has the ability to be free cash flow positive at all points of the cycle; the company's financial position has improved, with an expected $26M reduction in net debt during Q4 and greater than $150M in available liquidity; and Alec Meikle's elevation to President is "a superb move [that] demonstrates the company's bench strength.

The analyst kept Lithium Americas (LAC) at Sector Perform rating while rating the others higher, saying the company "can't benefit from higher pricing, as it doesn't produce lithium or revenue, and won't for several years... in scenarios where we see fly-up pricing over the next couple of years, why should LAC outperform if it can't benefit from participation in the fly-up pricing?"

data.title: Hertz Global slips after the Supreme Court declines to hear bond payout case
data.description:
The Supreme Court declined to hear an appeal from Hertz Global Holdings (HTZ) in relation to bond payments that were part of its Chapter 11 bankruptcy.

The SCOTUS decision not to hear the case means that a Third Circuit ruling that favors the company’s unsecured bondholders will remain in place.  Hertz (HTZ) will be required to honor the appeals court’s conclusion that it must pay bondholders contractual “make‑whole” premiums and additional interest under its pre‑bankruptcy indentures. 

As a result, Hertz (HTZ) is considered to be on the hook for more than about $270 million in extra amounts to affected bondholders, on top of principal.

Shares of Hertz Global (HTZ) were 2.7% lower in Monday afternoon trading.

data.title: Blackstone moves to exit U.K. rental home platform Leaf Living - report
data.description:
Blackstone (BX) has tapped Goldman Sachs (GS) and CBRE Group (CBRE) to advise on a potential sale of its U.K. rental home business, Leaf Living,

The platform is expected to fetch about £1B ($1.3B) in a deal, according to a media report on Monday, Bloomberg reported, citing people familiar with the matter.

Leaf Living was launched in 2021 as a joint venture between Blackstone (BX) and Regis Group to manage newly-built single-family rental homes across the U.K.

The move comes shortly after U.S. President Donald Trump dealt a blow to corporate landlords, saying he will take steps to ban large institutional investors from buying single-family homes in his effort to make homes more affordable for Americans. Blackstone (BX) is a major SFH player in both the U.S. and Europe.

Blackstone (BX) did not immediately respond to Seeking Alpha's request for comment.

data.title: Sarepta guides Q4 sales below estimates
data.description:
Sarepta Therapeutics (SRPT) estimates total net product revenue of $369.6M ($380.61M estimated) for Q4 and $1.86B ($2.12B estimated) for full-year 2025.

Elevidys (delandistrogene moxeparvovec), the firm’s gene therapy indicated for the treatment of ambulatory patients 4 years of age and older with Duchenne muscular dystrophy, is estimated to have generated net product revenue of $110.4M in Q4 and $898.7M for the full year 2025.

Preliminary year-end cash balance is projected at approximately $953.8M.

In November 2025, the U.S. FDA ordered a boxed warning to be added to Elevidys’ label, citing the risk of fatal acute liver failure following patient deaths. Sarepta (SRPT) reported the deaths of two teenage boys who were non-ambulatory who used the therapy, first in March and then in June.

In July, the company said a third patient who was taking part in an early-stage trial for a new treatment for limb-girdle muscular dystrophy died from acute liver failure.

CEO Doug Ingram said: “Sarepta faced and overcame challenges in 2025, achieving meaningful pipeline progress and solid performance. […] We have important initiatives in 2026 to continue the success of our approved therapies, particularly ELEVIDYS. We previously set a yearly sales floor for ELEVIDYS of $500.0 million. We reconfirm that floor and our intentions to exceed it. However, we will stop short of providing more detailed guidance until we begin to evaluate the results of our initiatives. We enter 2026 on a strong financial footing, with significant opportunities with our four approved therapies and our exciting siRNA pipeline.”

SRPT shares were down over 14% at $20.40 at press time.

data.title: UK to bring into force law against companies providing tools for AI deepfakes: report
data.description:
The U.K. is set to bring into force a law — perhaps as soon as this week — that will make it illegal for companies to provide tools for artificial intelligence deepfakes, the BBC reported.

Concerns have risen recently in the country regarding x.AI's (X.AI) Grok chatbot, following a growing outcry over its use for sexualized deepfakes of women and children. On Friday, xAI said it restricted the use of AI service Grok's image generation features to paying subscribers.

Technology Secretary Liz Kendall told the House of Commons that the law would prevent the tools from being released, the news outlet added. Secretary Kendall also said the aforementioned images were not “harmless,” but rather “weapons of abuse.”

“Legacy Media Les,” the Elon Musk-led xAI said in an automated response to Seeking Alpha regarding the report.

In response to the outrage, Musk took to X on Jan. 3 and wrote that “anyone using Grok to make illegal content will suffer the same consequences as if they upload illegal content.”

On Monday, the U.K. online safety watchdog Ofcom launched an investigation into Musk's X to determine if the company complied with its duties to protect people in the U.K. from content that is illegal in the country.

“There have been deeply concerning reports of the Grok AI chatbot account on X being used to create and share undressed images of people – which may amount to intimate image abuse or pornography – and sexualized images of children that may amount to child sexual abuse material (CSAM),” Ofcom said in a press release. Formerly known as Twitter, X merged with x.AI in March 2025.

The issue has raised concerns on both sides of the Atlantic. A group of U.S. senators recently asked Apple (AAPL) and Alphabet's (GOOG) (GOOGL) Google to remove the X and Grok apps from their respective app stores, citing the generation of nonconsensual sexualized images of women and children on the platforms.

data.title: Wall Street hunts for clear direction amid Powell investigation
data.description:
Wall Street trades mixed on Monday after reports that the U.S. attorney’s office has opened a criminal investigation into Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell.

The tech-focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was up by 0.3%. At the same time, the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was near even, and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) fell 0.2%. 

From a sector vantage point, six of the S&P 500’s 11 sectors were in negative trading territory with Financials suffering the most.  At the other end of the spectrum, Consumer Staples have been the strongest performer on the session.

The investigation reportedly centers on the central bank’s renovation of its Washington headquarters and whether Powell lied to Congress about the project’s scope, according to the New York Times. 

Powell released an extraordinary statement, asserting that grand jury subpoenas served by the Department of Justice are not about legitimate legal concerns but rather punishment for the central bank’s refusal to set interest rates according to President Donald Trump’s preferences. 

U.S. Treasury yields inched higher across the curve. The shorter-end U.S. 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) moved up 1 basis point to 3.54%, while the U.S.10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) climbed 1 basis point to 4.17%. 

The economic calendar is light, with investors eyeing inflation data later this week in the form of the consumer price index and the producer price index. 

“We expect Tuesday’s CPI report to show inflation holding steady in the 2.7% range. While we may see a slight 'pop' in monthly goods prices due to ongoing tariff pass-throughs, the broader trend is clearly drifting toward our 2026 target of 2.7%–2.9%,” Robert Edwards, chief investment officer of Edwards Asset Management said. 

“2026 is likely to be a sawtooth year for the markets, where stocks experience a 7-15% pullback in the first half of the year for the simple reason that too much of Wall Street is bullish. The market is due for a sentiment reset and we expect that reset to take place in the next 6-months. This sentiment reset is necessary fuel for the next leg of the bull market. We expect stocks to reach record highs by year-end,” Edwards added.

data.title: Sen. Murkowski joins Tillis on backing Fed independence
data.description:
Senator Lisa Murkowski (R-AK) agreed with her Republican colleague Senator Thom Tillis of North Carolina that it's "right" to block any Federal Reserve nominees until the matter of the administration's investigation into Fed Chair Jerome Powell is resolved.

On Sunday, the New York Times reported that the Department of Justice started a criminal investigation into Powell over the central bank's renovation project and whether the Fed chair had lied to Congress about the project's scope. Powell, in a rare response to political attacks, said the probe is a pretext for pressuring the Fed about its rate policy.

"After speaking with Chair Powell this morning, it's clear the administration's investigation is nothing more than an attempt at coercion," she wrote in a post on X on Monday. "If the Federal Reserve loses its independence, the stability of our markets and the broader economy will suffer."

She recommended that if the Department of Justice feels an investigation into Powell is warranted based on project cost overruns — which aren't unusual — then Congress should investigate the Department of Justice.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Trump Administration nears trade deal with Taiwan; TSM ticks up: report
data.description:
The Trump Administration is working on a new trade deal with Taiwan, which includes Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM), the world's largest chipmaker, building more facilities in the U.S., according to the New York Times.

TSMC shares were up 1.4% during early afternoon trading on Monday.

The new agreement, which has been negotiated for months, would reduce the U.S. tariff rate to 15% for goods exported from the island nation, the report said. The deal would also require TSMC to build five more manufacturing sites in Arizona.

Imports from Taiwan are currently at a 20% tariff rate. However, semiconductors and other electronic products have been exempt due to national security issues, placing them under Section 232, which is part of the Trade Expansion Act of 1962. They have been under a separate investigation by the U.S. Chamber of Commerce for about a year. 

The Trump Administration has indicated in the past that it might exclude companies from Section 232 tariffs if they significantly invest in U.S. facilities.
